feed.title: Boogers and Poop
feed.subtitle: One dad's adventures in twin parenting.

entry.title: Welcome to Jekyll!
entry.content:
10987654322Test. You’ll find this post in your _posts directory. Go ahead and edit it and re-build the site to see your changes. You can rebuild the site in many different ways, but the most common way is to run jekyll serve, which launches a web server and auto-regenerates your site when a file is updated.

To add new posts, simply add a file in the _posts directory that follows the convention YYYY-MM-DD-name-of-post.ext and includes the necessary front matter. Take a look at the source for this post to get an idea about how it works.

Jekyll also offers powerful support for code snippets:

Check out the Jekyll docs for more info on how to get the most out of Jekyll. File all bugs/feature requests at Jekyll’s GitHub repo. If you have questions, you can ask them on Jekyll Talk.
entry.summary: 10987654322Test. You’ll find this post in your _posts directory. Go ahead and edit it and re-build the site to see your changes. You can rebuild the site in many different ways, but the most common way is to run jekyll serve, which launches a web server and auto-regenerates your site when a file is updated.

entry.title: Tonight it happened
entry.content:
one baby ate the other baby’s poop.

After the bath, one baby did a sneaky poop in the corner while parental attention was on the other.  Then while pooper was being put to bed, the other discovered the a culinary experience, and at the time of discovery was in the process of spreading it around.

Oh yeah, and both babies can walk.
entry.summary: one baby ate the other baby’s poop.

entry.title: Plane travel
entry.content:
Ah the joys of traveling with babies.

Today, a diaper leaked while on the plane, and I got to sit in baby pee. In fact, I’m still sitting in baby pee, 2 hours later. And since the Airbnb checkin isn’t for another 3 hours, I should be well marinated by the time I get a shower.

Truly parenting is sublime.
entry.summary: Ah the joys of traveling with babies.

entry.title: 11 months!
entry.content:
Today, the babies are 11 months old. To celebrate, they broke into the pantry.

At this point, they can both stand up independently, though L has been practicing a lot more than Z. They both cruise, but no one has taken an independent first step. They understand “dance” & “clap”, they can (sort of) say “cat”, and C is convinced that they’re learning “Mama”. Latest trick is doing “aaaaaah” while we tap their faces.
entry.summary: Today, the babies are 11 months old. To celebrate, they broke into the pantry.

entry.title: Sniffing butts
entry.content:
Never before having babies did I ever voluntarily stick my nose in someones butt and sniff. Now? When that tell-tale scent wafts through the room, I promptly grab the nearest baby and take a whiff. It’s weird.

Relatedly, I’ve never been so happy when it turns out that someone farted.
entry.summary: Never before having babies did I ever voluntarily stick my nose in someones butt and sniff. Now? When that tell-tale scent wafts through the room, I promptly grab the nearest baby and take a whiff. It’s weird.

entry.title: Baby learning vs machine learning
entry.content:
While on paternity leave, I’m boning up on machine learning as a side interest. Watching babies learn at the same time provides some really interesting comparisons.

I’m particularly struck by how babies learn new physical skills like standing and walking, and all of the failed attempts they go through. This seems somehow similar to the process of training neural nets. In contrast, you can also see them learn new categories of things from a single example (e.g. food I like). The speed with which they generalize is astounding!
entry.summary: While on paternity leave, I’m boning up on machine learning as a side interest. Watching babies learn at the same time provides some really interesting comparisons.

entry.title: Get a job, babies!
entry.content:
Baby modeling.

People don’t just wake up one day as fully formed capitalists; you’ve got to get them indoctrinated early to ensure they fully embrace the values of crass consumerism.  So, when someone posted on Nextdoor that they were looking for baby models for a clothing line, I went right ahead and sacrificed the girls on the altar of commercialism.

Anyway, they were really cute, and scored some sweet onesies.

Onesies by Om Baby

Should you need baby models, you can contact their agent right here.

entry.title: PSA: When serving spicy food to babies, use a spoon.

link.title: PSA: When serving spicy food to babies, use a spoon.

entry.content:
We have a pretty liberal approach to food: babies eat basically what we do.  We like spicy food, so naturally, we feed it to the babies. We were feeding them some spicy spaghetti sauce, and they were loving it, until the moment when a hand went from shoveling food into mouth, to rubbing eyes…

So yeah, lesson learned: use a spoon when serving spicy food.
entry.summary: We have a pretty liberal approach to food: babies eat basically what we do. We like spicy food, so naturally, we feed it to the babies. We were feeding them some spicy spaghetti sauce, and they were loving it, until the moment when a hand went from shoveling food into mouth, to rubbing eyes…

entry.title: Another milestone: standing up
entry.content: They’ve evolved! From squirming, wriggly grubs, they have matured into magnificent hind-leg-standers. Unfettered by the need to support themselves with their hands, they can freely employ them to pick up and chew on dirty laundry and sandals.
entry.summary: They’ve evolved! From squirming, wriggly grubs, they have matured into magnificent hind-leg-standers. Unfettered by the need to support themselves with their hands, they can freely employ them to pick up and chew on dirty laundry and sandals.

entry.title: My babies sound like zombies
entry.content: Why did no one ever tell me that babies sound like zombies? It always sounds like there’s an imminent brain-feast.
entry.summary: Why did no one ever tell me that babies sound like zombies? It always sounds like there’s an imminent brain-feast.
